news_summary: Recent advancements in immunotherapy and innovative treatments show promise for combating rare cancers, while new insights into memory improvement and vitamin deficiencies offer hope for healthier aging.
news_summary_fr: Les progrès récents en matière d'immunothérapie et de traitements innovants semblent prometteurs dans la lutte contre les cancers rares, tandis que les nouvelles découvertes concernant l'amélioration de la mémoire et les carences en vitamines laissent entrevoir la perspective d'un vieillissement en meilleure santé.
news_summary_es: Los recientes avances en inmunoterapia y los tratamientos innovadores se presentan prometedores para combatir los cánceres raros, mientras que los nuevos conocimientos sobre la mejora de la memoria y las carencias vitamínicas ofrecen esperanza para un envejecimiento más saludable.

articles.title: FDA-approved drug may finally help immunotherapy defeat rare liver cancer
articles.summary: Researchers found that a rare liver cancer evades immunotherapy by luring immune T cells away from the tumor and trapping them in nearby fibrous tissue. An FDA-approved drug called AMD3100 freed those T cells to attack the cancer, significantly improving the effectiveness of immunotherapy in tumor samples.
articles.content:
A rare and aggressive form of liver cancer has long resisted immunotherapy, a treatment that helps the body's own immune system attack cancer cells. Now, researchers have identified a potential way to overcome that resistance using an FDA-approved drug that is already available for another medical condition.

The findings suggest a possible new treatment strategy for fibrolamellar carcinoma, a rare liver cancer that primarily affects children and young adults.

Fibrolamellar carcinoma makes up about 2% of all liver cancer cases. There is currently no cure, and the disease is often discovered only after it has spread to other parts of the body, leaving many patients with limited treatment options and poor survival prospects.

How Fibrolamellar Carcinoma Evades the Immune System

The study, published in the journal Gastroenterology, sheds light on why immunotherapy has struggled against this cancer.

Researchers found that fibrolamellar tumors alter their surrounding environment in a way that prevents immune T cells from reaching the cancer. Instead of moving into the tumor and attacking cancer cells, the T cells become trapped elsewhere. This process, known as T-cell exclusion, effectively keeps the immune system from carrying out its normal cancer-fighting role.

The team also discovered that AMD3100, a drug already approved by the U.S. Food and Drug Administration for another disorder, can disrupt this process. By preventing tumors from trapping T cells, the drug allows those immune cells to reach and attack the cancer.

"Our results provide among the first indications of why a type of immunotherapy called immune checkpoint inhibition hasn't worked well in these patients, and even if this particular drug isn't the end-all-be-all, it teaches us that this T-cell exclusion phenomenon is an important one to tackle in fibrolamellar carcinoma," said Praveen Sethupathy '03, professor of physiological genomics and chair of the Department of Biomedical Sciences in the College of Veterinary Medicine.

Sethupathy served as co-senior author of the study alongside Dr. Venu Pillarisetty, a surgical oncologist at the University of Washington.

Advanced Technology Reveals the Tumor Environment

To better understand what was happening inside these tumors, researchers used a powerful technique known as single-nucleus transcriptomics.

This technology allowed the team to isolate the nucleus of individual cells within tumor tissue and determine which genes were active in each cell. The approach provided an unprecedented view of the tumor microenvironment and the interactions taking place inside it.

"It wasn't until we were able to use this technology that the picture of the tumor microenvironment began to clear up for us," said Andreas Stephanou, a co-first author on the study and a Cornell graduate student co-mentored by Sethupathy and Iwijn de Vlaminck, associate professor in the Meinig School of Biomedical Engineering in the Duffield College of Engineering.

Why Immunotherapy Sometimes Fails

Immune checkpoint inhibitors work by activating the body's own T cells and encouraging them to move into tumors where they can destroy cancer cells.

These therapies have produced major benefits in several cancers, including liver, lung, kidney, and bladder cancers, as well as melanoma. However, many other cancers, including pancreatic, prostate, and brain cancers, often do not respond well to these treatments.

The researchers say that features of the tumor microenvironment, including T-cell exclusion, may help explain why some cancers remain resistant to immune checkpoint inhibitors.

The Role of Fibrous Bands in Tumors

Fibrolamellar carcinoma gets its name from the thick fibrous bands that run throughout the tumors.

"Despite all of the recent advances in the study of this cancer, we still haven't pinpointed how these fibrous bands contribute, if at all, to the tumor's progression," Stephanou said.

The researchers found that these bands are produced by stellate cells, which are normal liver cells that become altered by the cancer. Once changed, the stellate cells release fibrous proteins that build the characteristic bands within the tumor.

Using single-cell technology, the team discovered that these altered stellate cells also send signals to nearby T cells. Those signals direct the immune cells away from cancer cells and toward the fibrous bands, where the T cells become trapped.

AMD3100 Restores Immune Cell Access

"So, then we asked, what if we were to block this signaling in T cells with a compound?" Sethupathy said.

To test that idea, researchers in the Pillarisetty laboratory at the University of Washington used slices of patient tumor tissue and treated them with AMD3100.

The results showed that the drug successfully guided T cells back into the center of the tumors. When AMD3100 was combined with immune checkpoint inhibition, T-cell activation increased even further, resulting in a significant rise in tumor cell death.

The researchers are now seeking liver cancer specialists interested in launching clinical trials to evaluate the treatment approach in patients.

"A compelling feature of this work is that AMD3100 is already FDA-approved, which can reduce risks and potentially speed up timelines for clinical trials in fibrolamellar carcinoma," Sethupathy said.

Co-first authors of the study were Jason Carter and Lindsey Dickerson, both members of the Pillarisetty laboratory at the University of Washington. Bo Shui, a senior research associate in the Sethupathy laboratory, was also a co-author.

The research was supported by funding from the Fibrolamellar Cancer Foundation.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant medical breakthrough where an FDA-approved drug, AMD3100, has been found to improve immunotherapy effectiveness against a rare and aggressive liver cancer by overcoming T-cell exclusion. This discovery offers hope for a new treatment strategy for fibrolamellar carcinoma, a cancer with limited current options and poor prognosis, potentially benefiting many patients including children and young adults. The study is detailed, focused on a single topic, and highlights a clear positive real-world impact with broad significance for cancer treatment.
articles.personality_title: FDA-approved drug helps immunotherapy fight rare liver cancer
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Fibrolamellar carcinoma is a rare and aggressive liver cancer affecting mostly children and young adults. It makes up about 2% of liver cancer cases and currently has no cure. This cancer often spreads before it is discovered, limiting treatment options and leading to poor survival rates.

**What happened** – Researchers found that this cancer tricks the immune system by trapping T cells, a type of immune cell, in thick fibrous tissue around the tumor. This stops T cells from attacking the cancer. They tested an FDA-approved drug called AMD3100 on tumor samples. The drug freed the trapped T cells, allowing them to enter the tumor and kill cancer cells. When combined with immunotherapy, this treatment made the immune attack much stronger.

**Impact** – This discovery explains why immunotherapy has not worked well for this cancer before. By blocking the trapping of T cells, AMD3100 allows the immune system to do its job. Since AMD3100 is already approved for other uses, it could reach patients faster than brand-new drugs. This offers new hope for better treatments against a cancer that currently has very few options.

**What's next step** – The researchers are looking for liver cancer doctors to start clinical trials. These trials will test if the drug combination works safely and effectively in patients. If successful, this could become a new treatment for fibrolamellar carcinoma.

**One-sentence takeaway** – An existing FDA-approved drug, AMD3100, may help immunotherapy overcome immune cell trapping and better fight a rare liver cancer.
articles.personality_title_fr: Un médicament approuvé par la FDA aide l’immunothérapie contre un cancer rare du foie
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Le carcinome fibrolamellaire est un cancer rare et agressif du foie qui touche principalement les enfants et les jeunes adultes. Il représente environ 2 % des cas de cancer du foie et n’a actuellement pas de traitement curatif. Ce cancer est souvent découvert après sa propagation, ce qui limite les options de traitement et réduit les chances de survie.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Les chercheurs ont découvert que ce cancer piège les cellules T, un type de cellules immunitaires, dans une épaisse couche de tissu fibreux autour de la tumeur. Cela empêche les cellules T d’attaquer le cancer. Ils ont testé un médicament appelé AMD3100, déjà approuvé par la FDA, sur des échantillons de tumeur. Ce médicament a libéré les cellules T piégées, leur permettant de pénétrer dans la tumeur et de tuer les cellules cancéreuses. Combiné à l’immunothérapie, ce traitement a renforcé la réponse du système immunitaire.

**Impact** – Cette découverte explique pourquoi l’immunothérapie n’a pas bien fonctionné jusqu’à présent contre ce cancer. En empêchant le piège des cellules T, AMD3100 aide le système immunitaire à combattre la maladie. Comme AMD3100 est déjà utilisé pour d’autres maladies, il pourrait être disponible plus rapidement pour les patients. Cela offre de nouvelles perspectives pour un cancer avec peu d’options.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs cherchent des médecins spécialistes du foie pour lancer des essais cliniques. Ces essais testeront si cette combinaison de médicaments est sûre et efficace chez les patients. En cas de succès, ce traitement pourrait devenir une nouvelle option pour le carcinome fibrolamellaire.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Le médicament AMD3100, déjà approuvé, pourrait aider l’immunothérapie à surmonter le piège des cellules immunitaires et mieux combattre un cancer rare du foie.
articles.personality_title_es: Medicamento aprobado por la FDA ayuda a la inmunoterapia contra un cáncer raro de hígado
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – El carcinoma fibrolamelar es un cáncer raro y agresivo del hígado que afecta principalmente a niños y adultos jóvenes. Representa alrededor del 2 % de los casos de cáncer de hígado y actualmente no tiene cura. Este cáncer a menudo se detecta después de haberse extendido, lo que limita las opciones de tratamiento y reduce las probabilidades de supervivencia.

**Qué pasó** – Los investigadores descubrieron que este cáncer atrapa a las células T, un tipo de célula del sistema inmunológico, en un tejido fibroso grueso alrededor del tumor. Esto impide que las células T ataquen el cáncer. Probaron un medicamento aprobado por la FDA llamado AMD3100 en muestras de tumor. El medicamento liberó las células T atrapadas, permitiéndoles entrar al tumor y destruir las células cancerosas. Combinado con la inmunoterapia, el tratamiento fortaleció la respuesta del sistema inmunitario.

**Impacto** – Este hallazgo explica por qué la inmunoterapia no ha funcionado bien antes contra este cáncer. Al bloquear la trampa para las células T, AMD3100 ayuda al sistema inmunológico a combatir el cáncer. Como AMD3100 ya está aprobado para otros usos, podría llegar a los pacientes más rápido. Esto abre nuevas posibilidades para un cáncer con pocas opciones.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores buscan médicos especialistas en cáncer de hígado para iniciar ensayos clínicos. Estos ensayos probarán si la combinación de medicamentos es segura y eficaz en pacientes. Si tiene éxito, podría convertirse en un nuevo tratamiento para el carcinoma fibrolamelar.

**Resumen en una frase** – El medicamento AMD3100, ya aprobado, podría ayudar a la inmunoterapia a superar la trampa de las células inmunitarias y combatir mejor un cáncer raro de hígado.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a vibrant liver-shaped landscape where glowing, determined T-cell-like orbs break free from thick, fibrous band-like barriers, flowing toward a radiant core symbolizing the tumor, with a gentle stream of light representing the FDA-approved drug AMD3100 guiding their path and unlocking the immune system’s power.

articles.title: Phages could enable us to hijack vaccine immunity to kill cancer cells
articles.summary: Phages, viruses that infect bacteria, could be genetically manipulated to destroy cancerous cells using the immunity we have acquired from vaccines
articles.content:
Phages, viruses that infect bacteria, could be genetically manipulated to destroy cancerous cells using the immunity we have acquired from vaccines

Transmission electron micrograph of Escherichia coli cells infected by phages (green dots) M.MAEDER/DEPT. OF MICROBIOLOGY, BIOZENTRUM/ SCIENCE PHOTO LIBRARY

Bacteria-killing viruses have destroyed cancer cells in mice by redirecting the immunity they built up from vaccinations to attack tumours. Vaccinating mice against malaria and then using a harmless phage, a virus that infects bacteria, to direct that immune response towards cancer cells resulted in the tumours being eradicated in nearly half of the animals.

Immunotherapy, treatments that use our immune system to fight cancer, has transformed how we treat some forms of the condition, but many people still don’t benefit. One of the major challenges is helping the immune system to recognise tumours as a target.

Looking for a way around this, Amin Hajitou at Imperial College London and his colleagues focused on a phage that usually infects E. coli. After attaching to these bacteria and injecting its genetic material, the phage hijacks their genetic machinery to produce thousands of new phages, then destroys the bacteria.

The researchers genetically engineered this phage to recognise and bind to proteins called αvβ3 and αvβ5 integrins, which are abundant on many tumour cells and largely absent from healthy ones. They also modified its genetic cargo to deliver instructions for producing a malaria-specific antigen, a molecular signal the immune system recognises as foreign. “The phage acts like a targeted delivery vehicle,” says Hajitou.

The team then tested this approach in 60 mice with cancerous tumours just beneath their skin. Fifteen of them were given a malaria vaccine and two weeks later, were injected with the phages in their tails, receiving six injections in total over the next two weeks. The rest acted as controls, with 15 receiving no intervention, 15 being given the malaria vaccine alone and 15 getting only the engineered phages.

Free newsletter Sign up to Eight Weeks to a Healthier You Your science-backed guide to the easy habits that will help you sleep well, stress less, eat smarter and age better. Sign up to newsletter

In 44 per cent of the treated mice, the tumours were eradicated and hadn’t returned a year later, when the study ended. Other treated mice lived longer than the control groups, which experienced no survival benefit.

“These modified viruses can be administered systemically into the mouse and they then specifically find out the tumour cells and infect these,” says David Withers at the University of Oxford. “This really improves upon current approaches to manipulate tumours, such as oncolytic viruses [which infect and destroy cancer cells], which require injections directly into the cancer, thus limiting where you can treat and, in the case of metastatic disease [when cancer spreads], [it’s] an impossible task trying to target all of the cancer sites.”

Adjusting the phage’s antigen-making instructions should mean that the approach also works when someone has been vaccinated against infections like seasonal flu or covid-19. “Other vaccines, stronger than malaria, should work even better,” says Hajitou. “The principle is to exploit pre-existing immune memory, [it’s] not something unique to malaria.”

The researchers are in talks with the Medicines and Healthcare Products Regulatory Agency (MHRA) in the UK about assessing the approach in an early-stage trial in people, which they hope to begin next year.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough where genetically engineered phages redirect vaccine-acquired immunity to destroy cancer cells, demonstrating tumor eradication in nearly half of treated mice and potential for broad application across various vaccines. This represents a meaningful advancement in cancer immunotherapy with promising implications for future human trials.
articles.personality_title: Engineered viruses use vaccine immunity to fight cancer in mice
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists are searching for new ways to help the immune system recognize and attack cancer cells. Traditional immunotherapy has limits because many tumors avoid detection by the immune system.

**What happened** – Researchers at Imperial College London modified a type of virus called a phage, which normally infects bacteria. They changed it to target proteins found mostly on cancer cells and to carry a signal from the malaria vaccine. When mice were vaccinated against malaria and then given these engineered phages, nearly half of their tumors disappeared.

**Impact** – This method uses the immune memory from vaccines to fight cancer, which is a new idea. Unlike some treatments that need direct injections into tumors, these phages can travel through the body and find cancer cells on their own. This could make it easier to treat cancers that have spread.

**What’s next step** – The team plans to test this approach in human clinical trials, possibly starting next year. They also believe this method could work with other vaccines, like flu or COVID-19 vaccines, making it widely useful.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Scientists have designed viruses that use vaccine-trained immunity to find and destroy cancer cells in mice, opening the door for new cancer treatments.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des virus modifiés utilisent l’immunité des vaccins pour combattre le cancer chez la souris
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques cherchent de nouvelles façons d’aider le système immunitaire à reconnaître et attaquer les cellules cancéreuses. L’immunothérapie classique a des limites car de nombreuses tumeurs échappent à la détection.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de l’Imperial College de Londres ont modifié un type de virus appelé phage, qui infecte normalement les bactéries. Ils l’ont programmé pour cibler des protéines présentes surtout sur les cellules cancéreuses et pour porter un signal du vaccin contre le paludisme. Quand des souris vaccinées contre le paludisme ont reçu ces phages modifiés, près de la moitié de leurs tumeurs ont disparu.

**Impact** – Cette méthode utilise la mémoire immunitaire des vaccins pour combattre le cancer, ce qui est une idée nouvelle. Contrairement à certains traitements nécessitant des injections directes dans la tumeur, ces phages peuvent circuler dans le corps et trouver seuls les cellules cancéreuses. Cela pourrait faciliter le traitement des cancers qui se sont propagés.

**Prochaine étape** – L’équipe prévoit d’essayer cette méthode dans des essais cliniques humains, dès l’année prochaine. Ils pensent aussi que cela pourrait fonctionner avec d’autres vaccins comme ceux contre la grippe ou le COVID-19, ce qui la rendrait très utile.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Des virus conçus pour utiliser l’immunité créée par les vaccins peuvent détecter et détruire les cellules cancéreuses chez la souris, ouvrant la voie à de nouveaux traitements contre le cancer.
articles.personality_title_es: Virus modificados usan la inmunidad de vacunas para combatir el cáncer en ratones
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos buscan nuevas formas de ayudar al sistema inmunológico a reconocer y atacar las células cancerosas. La inmunoterapia tradicional tiene límites porque muchos tumores evaden la detección.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores del Imperial College de Londres modificaron un tipo de virus llamado fagos, que normalmente infectan bacterias. Lo cambiaron para que atacara proteínas que se encuentran principalmente en células cancerosas y para llevar una señal del vacuna contra la malaria. Cuando ratones vacunados contra la malaria recibieron estos fagos modificados, casi la mitad de sus tumores desaparecieron.

**Impacto** – Este método usa la memoria inmunológica creada por las vacunas para luchar contra el cáncer, una idea nueva. A diferencia de algunos tratamientos que requieren inyecciones directas en los tumores, estos fagos pueden viajar por el cuerpo y encontrar las células cancerosas por sí solos. Esto podría facilitar el tratamiento de cánceres que se han extendido.

**Próximo paso** – El equipo planea probar este método en ensayos clínicos con humanos, posiblemente el próximo año. También creen que podría funcionar con otras vacunas, como las de la gripe o COVID-19, haciéndolo muy útil.

**Resumen en una frase** – Científicos diseñaron virus que usan la inmunidad entrenada por vacunas para localizar y destruir células cancerosas en ratones, abriendo nuevas opciones para tratamientos contra el cáncer.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting showing a stylized, glowing phage virus shaped like a key unlocking and gently enveloping a cluster of abstract cancer cells marked by distinctive αvβ3 and αvβ5 integrin patterns, while a subtle halo of vaccine-inspired molecular symbols surrounds the scene, symbolizing the immune system’s redirected attack on tumors.

articles.title: They knew the pill was fake but their memory still improved
articles.summary: Healthy older adults experienced measurable improvements in memory, physical performance, and stress after taking placebo pills for just three weeks. The most surprising finding was that the placebo often worked even when participants knew the pills were completely inactive.
articles.content:
A placebo, or fake supplement, may offer real benefits for older adults, according to new research from psychologists at the Università Cattolica in Milan. After taking placebo pills for three weeks, participants showed improvements in both physical performance and cognitive function. Surprisingly, the benefits were seen even when participants knew the pills contained no active ingredients.

The study, published in the International Journal of Clinical and Health Psychology, was led by Diletta Barbiani, Alessandro Antonietti, and Francesco Pagnini. It was supported by PNRR grants through the Age-IT project.

"The study is part of an established line of research in which we analyze the role of the mind in aging processes, which is very important," says Pagnini, Full Professor of Clinical Psychology at the Faculty of Psychology of the Università Cattolica.

Testing the Placebo Effect in Healthy Aging

Until now, no research had investigated whether a traditional placebo could influence abilities that naturally decline with age.

"Our goal," Professor Pagnini explains, "was to clarify whether an open-label placebo therapy (i.e., where the recipient is aware it is a placebo) or a fake supplement (people don't know it's a placebo) could influence psychological, cognitive, and physical functions in older adults living in the community."

To explore that question, the researchers recruited 90 healthy older adults and randomly assigned them to one of three groups. One group received no treatment at all. A second group received placebo pills but was told the pills contained active ingredients designed to improve well-being and physical function. The third group received the same inactive pills but was openly informed that the pills were placebos that could still trigger beneficial mind-body responses.

Before and after the three-week study, participants completed questionnaires (providing information on levels of perceived stress, psychological well-being, sleepiness, fatigue, optimism, self-efficacy, and stereotypes about aging). They also took objective tests measuring short-term memory, selective attention, and physical performance.

Memory, Stress, and Physical Performance Improved

After three weeks, the participants who knowingly took placebo pills experienced lower stress levels than both the deceptive placebo group and the control group. They also showed significant improvements in short-term memory compared with those who received no intervention.

Overall, both placebo groups experienced gains in cognitive and physical performance, although the strongest improvements were generally seen among participants who knew they were taking a placebo.

Physical performance increased by 7% in the deceptive placebo group and by 9.2% in the open-label placebo group. Cognitive performance also improved. Depending on the specific test, scores increased by between 12.6% and 14.6% among participants who believed they were taking a real supplement, while those who knowingly took a placebo improved by between 6.9% and 21.5%.

"These are significant effects," the psychologist emphasizes, "comparable to those seen in some experimental studies on physical activity regarding physical performance and cognitive training, especially with regard to memory."

Researchers also observed reductions in drowsiness. Stress levels improved most noticeably among participants who were aware they were taking a placebo.

A New Approach to Healthy Aging?

The findings suggest that placebo treatments can improve several aspects of functioning in older adults, with open-label placebos performing as well as, or in some cases better than, deceptive placebos.

According to the researchers, this makes open-label placebos a promising and ethically acceptable strategy for supporting healthy aging.

Professor Pagnini says the results add to growing scientific evidence that the mind plays an important role in the aging process. Thoughts, emotions, and self-perception may influence not only psychological well-being but also physical abilities and cognitive function, highlighting the powerful connection between the mind and the body.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific finding that placebo pills, even when known to be inactive, can improve memory, physical performance, and stress in healthy older adults. This discovery has broad implications for healthy aging and mental health, offering a promising, ethical, and accessible intervention to enhance quality of life for a large demographic. The study is well-detailed and focused on a single impactful topic.
articles.personality_title: Placebo pills improve memory and strength in older adults, even when known to be fake
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists wanted to find out if placebo pills, which contain no real medicine, could help healthy older adults improve their memory, physical ability, and stress levels. Usually, placebo effects work when people think they are taking real medicine, but this study tested if knowing the pills were fake still made a difference.

**What happened** – Researchers from the Università Cattolica in Milan studied 90 healthy older adults. They split them into three groups: one took no pills, one took placebo pills but believed they were real supplements, and the last group took placebo pills while knowing they were fake. After three weeks, all participants were tested on memory, attention, physical performance, and stress.

**Impact** – The surprising result was that people who knew their pills were fake still showed better memory, less stress, and improved physical performance. Their improvements were sometimes even stronger than those who thought the pills were real. This shows the mind can boost body and brain health just by expecting benefits, even without real medicine.

**What's next step** – Researchers believe open-label placebo treatments (where people know the pills are fake) could become an ethical and easy way to help older adults stay sharper and healthier. More studies will explore how to use this approach in daily life and medical care.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Knowing a pill is fake doesn’t stop it from helping older adults improve memory, reduce stress, and increase physical strength.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les pilules placebo améliorent la mémoire et la force des personnes âgées, même lorsqu'elles savent qu'elles sont fausses
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Des scientifiques ont voulu savoir si les pilules placebo, qui ne contiennent pas de médicament réel, pouvaient aider les personnes âgées en bonne santé à améliorer leur mémoire, leurs capacités physiques et leur niveau de stress. Habituellement, l’effet placebo fonctionne lorsque les gens pensent prendre un vrai médicament, mais cette étude a testé si le fait de savoir que les pilules étaient fausses avait encore un effet.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de l’Università Cattolica de Milan ont étudié 90 personnes âgées en bonne santé. Ils les ont divisés en trois groupes : un groupe n’a pris aucune pilule, un autre a pris des pilules placebo en croyant qu’elles étaient de vrais compléments, et le dernier groupe a pris des pilules placebo en sachant qu’elles étaient fausses. Après trois semaines, tous les participants ont été testés sur la mémoire, l’attention, les performances physiques et le stress.

**Impact** – Le résultat surprenant est que les personnes qui savaient que leurs pilules étaient fausses ont tout de même montré une meilleure mémoire, moins de stress et une amélioration des performances physiques. Leurs progrès étaient parfois même plus forts que ceux qui pensaient que les pilules étaient réelles. Cela montre que l’esprit peut améliorer la santé du corps et du cerveau simplement en s’attendant à des bienfaits, même sans médicament réel.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs pensent que les traitements par placebo en connaissance de cause (où les gens savent que les pilules sont fausses) pourraient devenir une méthode éthique et facile pour aider les personnes âgées à rester plus alertes et en meilleure santé. D’autres études exploreront comment utiliser cette approche dans la vie quotidienne et les soins médicaux.

**En une phrase** – Savoir qu’une pilule est fausse n’empêche pas qu’elle aide les personnes âgées à améliorer leur mémoire, réduire leur stress et augmenter leur force physique.
articles.personality_title_es: Las pastillas placebo mejoran la memoria y la fuerza en adultos mayores, incluso cuando saben que son falsas
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos querían saber si las pastillas placebo, que no contienen medicina real, podían ayudar a los adultos mayores sanos a mejorar su memoria, capacidad física y niveles de estrés. Normalmente, el efecto placebo funciona cuando las personas creen que están tomando medicina verdadera, pero este estudio probó si saber que las pastillas eran falsas aún tenía efecto.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de la Università Cattolica en Milán estudiaron a 90 adultos mayores sanos. Los dividieron en tres grupos: uno no tomó pastillas, otro tomó pastillas placebo creyendo que eran suplementos reales, y el último grupo tomó pastillas placebo sabiendo que eran falsas. Después de tres semanas, todos fueron evaluados en memoria, atención, desempeño físico y estrés.

**Impacto** – El resultado sorprendente fue que las personas que sabían que sus pastillas eran falsas mostraron mejor memoria, menos estrés y mejor desempeño físico. Sus mejoras a veces fueron incluso mayores que las de quienes creían que las pastillas eran reales. Esto muestra que la mente puede mejorar la salud del cuerpo y cerebro solo con esperar beneficios, incluso sin medicina real.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores creen que los tratamientos con placebo abierto (donde las personas saben que las pastillas son falsas) podrían convertirse en una forma ética y sencilla de ayudar a los adultos mayores a mantenerse más ágiles y saludables. Más estudios explorarán cómo usar este método en la vida diaria y en la atención médica.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Saber que una pastilla es falsa no impide que ayude a los adultos mayores a mejorar la memoria, reducir el estrés y aumentar la fuerza física.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of three stylized, translucent pill capsules floating gently above an open, glowing human brain silhouette, surrounded by subtle, ascending light waves symbolizing improved memory and cognitive function, with soft, natural earth tones and calming blues evoking a serene, hopeful atmosphere.

articles.title: This common vitamin deficiency can mimic normal aging
articles.summary: Vitamin B12 is needed in microscopic amounts, but a shortage can have major effects on health and energy. The vitamin was first linked to a lifesaving liver treatment for pernicious anemia nearly 100 years ago. Today, researchers are finding that B12 may also help keep cellular powerhouses called mitochondria functioning properly. This could explain why some people experience fatigue and brain fog even before traditional signs of deficiency show up.
articles.content:
Two micrograms is an almost unimaginably small amount. It weighs less than a tiny fragment of a grain of table salt. Yet adults need only around this amount of vitamin B12 each day, depending on the guideline used, to support red blood cells, nerves and DNA production.

In 2026, it is 100 years since George Minot and William Murphy reported that a liver-rich diet could treat pernicious anemia, then a frequently fatal disease. Their work transformed medicine and eventually led scientists to identify vitamin B12 as the substance in liver that treated the disease.

But the route to that breakthrough began with an unexpected clue from animal experiments. The American physician and pathologist George Whipple had shown that liver helped dogs recover from anemia caused by blood loss. Blood-loss anemia happens when the body loses red blood cells through bleeding. Pernicious anemia is different: the problem is not bleeding, but poor absorption of vitamin B12. Even so, Whipple’s experiments pointed researchers towards liver as a source of a powerful blood-forming factor.

Patients with pernicious anemia who had been close to death often improved dramatically within weeks of eating liver-rich diets. The success of liver treatment eventually led scientists to isolate the deep red compound now known as vitamin B12, or cobalamin.

Often mistaken

Despite decades of research, vitamin B12 deficiency remains common, particularly among older adults, vegans, vegetarians and people with conditions that affect absorption. Some people do not consume enough B12 because it is naturally found mainly in foods from animals, including meat, fish, eggs and dairy products. Others struggle to absorb it properly.

This becomes more common with age. Some older people produce less stomach acid, which is needed to release B12 from food. Others develop autoimmune gastritis, in which the immune system damages stomach cells involved in producing acid and intrinsic factor, the protein needed for vitamin B12 absorption. Weight-loss surgery and some medicines used for diabetes or acid reflux can also reduce absorption.

The symptoms of deficiency can develop slowly and are often mistaken for normal ageing. People may feel exhausted, weak or short of breath. Some develop numbness or tingling in their hands and feet, poor balance, memory problems or what many describe as “brain fog”. These symptoms are not specific to B12 deficiency, so persistent tiredness, tingling or balance problems should be checked rather than assumed to be a simple vitamin problem.

People at higher risk, including vegans, vegetarians, older adults and those taking medicines that affect stomach acid or diabetes treatment, may need testing or supplementation advice from a health professional.

Doctors have traditionally linked tiredness in B12 deficiency to anemia. Without enough vitamin B12, the bone marrow cannot produce healthy red blood cells. Instead, it releases unusually large and immature cells that carry oxygen less effectively around the body.

But anemia may not be the only reason people with low B12 feel exhausted.

Low energy

In humans, vitamin B12 is directly needed by only two enzymes, the proteins that help chemical reactions happen in the body. One helps the body make DNA, which cells need when they divide. The other helps mitochondria process certain fats and protein building blocks. Mitochondria are the tiny structures inside cells that help turn food into usable energy.

This mitochondrial role has attracted growing interest from researchers studying ageing, muscle function and vitamin B12 status. A 2026 study explored what happens when cells do not have enough B12. Researchers found that low B12 could interfere with the DNA inside mitochondria and reduce energy production in laboratory models of skeletal muscle (muscle cells studied outside the human body).

A related study in aged female mice found that B12 supplementation improved several signs of mitochondrial health in muscle, including the number and structure of mitochondria. Together, this work points to one possible reason why some people with low B12 report fatigue before obvious anemia is detected.

These findings do not mean vitamin B12 supplements can reverse ageing or act as an energy booster for people whose B12 levels are already normal.

Scientists have suspected a link between B12 and mitochondrial function for many years, because one of the two B12-dependent enzymes works inside mitochondria. Earlier research has also suggested that low B12 status may be linked with poorer muscle function in older adults, although much of this work is observational and cannot prove cause and effect.

So if you’re feeling persistently tired, is it worth paying for vitamin B12 injections at a wellness clinic or medispa? For most people, no. B12 injections are an established treatment for diagnosed deficiency, particularly when absorption is impaired, and the NHS uses hydroxocobalamin injections for vitamin B12 deficiency anemia.

But there is little evidence that B12 shots boost energy, weight loss or performance in people whose B12 levels are already normal. The more useful first step is to find out what is causing the tiredness.

The story of vitamin B12 is unusual because the body needs so little of it, yet the consequences of deficiency can be profound. Long before scientists understood its chemistry, doctors recognized that something in liver could restore strength, appetite and vitality to desperately ill patients.

A century later, researchers are still finding that this tiny cobalt-containing molecule does more than prevent anemia. It may also help explain how cells maintain energy and function as the body ages.
articles.reasoning: The article highlights important health research on vitamin B12 deficiency, its impact on aging-related symptoms, and mitochondrial function. It provides meaningful insights into a common health issue affecting a broad population, especially older adults, and underscores the significance of proper diagnosis and treatment. The story is well-focused, informative, and offers hope for better understanding and managing fatigue and cognitive decline linked to B12 deficiency.
articles.personality_title: New research links vitamin B12 shortage to tiredness and aging symptoms
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Vitamin B12 is a tiny but essential nutrient needed to keep our blood, nerves, and DNA healthy. A century ago, doctors discovered that eating liver, rich in B12, could save patients from a deadly blood disease called pernicious anemia. Today, scientists study how B12 affects the tiny energy factories inside our cells, called mitochondria.

**What happened** – Recent studies in 2026 found that not having enough vitamin B12 can harm mitochondria, reducing the energy muscles and other cells produce. This might explain why some people feel very tired or have memory problems even before typical signs like anemia show up. Researchers tested this in muscle cells and aged mice, showing that B12 helps keep mitochondria healthy.

**Impact** – This discovery helps explain why B12 deficiency symptoms often look like normal aging, such as fatigue, weakness, and “brain fog.” It shows that tiredness from low B12 is not just about blood problems but also about how cells make energy. Since many older adults and people with certain diets or medical conditions may struggle to absorb enough B12, understanding this link is important for better diagnosis and treatment.

**What's next step** – Doctors may focus more on testing B12 levels in people with unexplained tiredness or memory issues, especially those at risk. Research will continue to explore how B12 supports mitochondria and whether treatments can help improve energy and muscle health in older adults. However, B12 supplements mainly help those who are deficient, not people with normal levels.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Low vitamin B12 can cause tiredness and aging-like symptoms by weakening the cell’s energy makers, showing the need for proper diagnosis and treatment in at-risk groups.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une nouvelle recherche relie la carence en vitamine B12 à la fatigue et aux symptômes du vieillissement
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – La vitamine B12 est un nutriment minuscule mais essentiel pour garder notre sang, nos nerfs et notre ADN en bonne santé. Il y a cent ans, les médecins ont découvert que manger du foie, riche en B12, pouvait sauver des patients d’une maladie du sang mortelle appelée anémie pernicieuse. Aujourd’hui, les scientifiques étudient comment la B12 affecte les petites usines d’énergie dans nos cellules, appelées mitochondries.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des études récentes en 2026 ont montré qu’un manque de vitamine B12 peut endommager les mitochondries, réduisant l’énergie produite par les muscles et d’autres cellules. Cela pourrait expliquer pourquoi certaines personnes se sentent très fatiguées ou ont des problèmes de mémoire avant même que des signes typiques comme l’anémie n’apparaissent. Les chercheurs ont testé cela sur des cellules musculaires et des souris âgées, montrant que la B12 aide à garder les mitochondries en bonne santé.

**Impact** – Cette découverte aide à comprendre pourquoi les symptômes de la carence en B12 ressemblent souvent au vieillissement normal, comme la fatigue, la faiblesse et le « brouillard cérébral ». Elle montre que la fatigue due à une faible B12 ne vient pas seulement des problèmes sanguins, mais aussi de la façon dont les cellules produisent de l’énergie. Comme de nombreux adultes plus âgés et certaines personnes ont du mal à absorber assez de B12, comprendre ce lien est important pour un meilleur diagnostic et traitement.

**Prochaine étape** – Les médecins pourraient davantage tester les niveaux de B12 chez les personnes fatiguées sans cause claire, surtout celles à risque. La recherche continuera d’explorer comment la B12 soutient les mitochondries et si des traitements peuvent améliorer l’énergie et la santé musculaire chez les personnes âgées. Cependant, les suppléments de B12 aident surtout ceux qui en manquent, pas ceux avec des niveaux normaux.

**Une phrase clé** – Une faible vitamine B12 peut causer fatigue et symptômes de vieillissement en affaiblissant les producteurs d’énergie des cellules, soulignant l’importance d’un bon diagnostic et traitement chez les personnes à risque.
articles.personality_title_es: Nueva investigación relaciona la falta de vitamina B12 con cansancio y síntomas de envejecimiento
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La vitamina B12 es un nutriente muy pequeño pero esencial para mantener saludables la sangre, los nervios y el ADN. Hace cien años, los médicos descubrieron que comer hígado, rico en B12, podía salvar a pacientes de una enfermedad mortal llamada anemia perniciosa. Hoy, los científicos estudian cómo la B12 afecta a las pequeñas fábricas de energía dentro de nuestras células, llamadas mitocondrias.

**Qué pasó** – Estudios recientes en 2026 encontraron que no tener suficiente vitamina B12 puede dañar las mitocondrias y reducir la energía que producen los músculos y otras células. Esto podría explicar por qué algunas personas se sienten muy cansadas o tienen problemas de memoria incluso antes de que aparezcan signos típicos como la anemia. Los investigadores probaron esto en células musculares y ratones viejos, mostrando que la B12 ayuda a mantener saludables las mitocondrias.

**Impacto** – Este descubrimiento ayuda a explicar por qué los síntomas de falta de B12 a menudo parecen envejecimiento normal, como fatiga, debilidad y “niebla mental”. Muestra que el cansancio por baja B12 no solo se debe a problemas en la sangre, sino también a cómo las células producen energía. Como muchos adultos mayores y personas con ciertas dietas o condiciones médicas tienen dificultad para absorber suficiente B12, entender esta conexión es importante para mejorar el diagnóstico y tratamiento.

**Próximo paso** – Los médicos podrían enfocarse más en medir los niveles de B12 en personas con cansancio o problemas de memoria sin causa clara, especialmente en quienes tienen riesgo. La investigación continuará explorando cómo la B12 apoya a las mitocondrias y si los tratamientos pueden mejorar la energía y la salud muscular en adultos mayores. Sin embargo, los suplementos de B12 ayudan principalmente a quienes tienen deficiencia, no a quienes tienen niveles normales.

**Una frase clave** – La baja vitamina B12 puede causar cansancio y síntomas similares al envejecimiento al debilitar las fábricas de energía celular, mostrando la importancia de un diagnóstico y tratamiento adecuados en grupos en riesgo.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a vibrant red liver glowing softly at the center, surrounded by delicate, stylized red blood cells and intricate, glowing blue mitochondria subtly intertwined like a protective network, all set against a gentle, natural background of soft earth tones and muted blues to symbolize vitality, energy, and the hidden power of vitamin B12 sustaining life.
